title: iPhone 17 vs 17 Pro after 1 Month - DON'T Be Fooled..
transcript: It's been about 7 weeks since Apple announced the new iPhone lineup, and the iPhone 17 is hands down the best base iPhone ever. Some would say it's finally Pro. But how good is it compared to the Pro and the Pro Max? And what downsides are there? Well, here are seven real differences after 7 weeks. Getting into real difference number one, it's the telephoto camera on the Pro models. And let me tell you, that is a massive difference. I use this camera all the time. And I've realized that when you have the base iPhone, you're missing that camera. There are so many things that you just cannot do. First of all, the portrait photos at 4x look absolutely amazing. This is like DSLR quality that you just can't get with that 1x or 2x mode on the base iPhone. It's also amazing for video when you're taking long range video of things happening further away because on the base iPhone you can only zoom in 6x from that 1x main camera and the quality becomes trash. This looks so much better with these pro models. It's actually usable. It looks amazing. But my favorite thing is using the telephoto camera as a viewfinder on the pro models. For example, I was at a recent funeral service and I actually used the telephoto to zoom in on a camera screen on a tripod to make sure that the battery was still good, make sure I had space on the SD card. So, I couldn't imagine not having the telephoto. But then on the flip side, for real difference number two, I cannot believe how pro the base iPhone 17 feels while you're using it. First of all, now that we have aluminum on the Pro models, it feels the same when holding them in the hands, but I just love how nice the display looks on the base 17. The bezels are ultra thin, same thickness as the Pro models. You have the always on display, which makes it feel like a pro phone. You have 120 Hz prootion for the first time on a base iPhone. It feels super smooth. The brightness, the display specs are all the same. the same ceramic shield too on the front. You have the same brand new selfie camera, which by the way is amazing. I love the new selfie cam. It is so useful in so many different ways, especially having landscape video and photos. You also have the same super fast charging speed for both wireless at 25 watts G2 and 40 watt for wired charging. I can't believe Apple put so many pro features. So, when you're using this, it feels like a pro phone dayto-day. Moving on to number three, we have the chip differences. Now, a lot of people think the A19 Pro is such a massive deal compared to the A19, but surprisingly, they both feel just as fast. They're so snappy in regular use, mostly because of the 120 Hz display that no longer feels like it's glitchy and slow. It's very nice and snappy. The old 60 Hz display was the main limitation on the iPhone 16, but now there's virtually no difference. Yes, you do have a RAM difference. 12 gigs on the Pros, 8 gigs on the base, but they feel generally the same. The only difference is you can open up more apps without them refreshing on the Pros, which is definitely nice. And then for the graphics performance, that's the biggest deal. 5 core versus six core. So, yes, if you look at the Geekbench numbers, you do see a performance jump there. But what about real gaming performance? But first, our sponsor ESR has their brand new 25 watt C2 magnetic car charger with cryobboost fan cooling to keep your iPhone cool. Its omni based design is super easy to install on a car vent using this nice convenient hook. And once it's attached, it doesn't block your air vent thanks to its flexible arm design. And with that, it's very stable while driving, even on bumpy roads. What's nice is that it comes with a 58 watt power adapter in the box, letting you charge two devices at once. And as far as the charging speeds, it is very fast, letting you charge a dead iPhone 17 Pro up to 60% in just 30 minutes, compared to Apple's Magsafe charger that only gets you to 50% in the same time. And the only way it can do this is with that built-in cryobboost fan that keeps your phone from overheating. This has saved me on multiple occasions when I'm leaving my home in a hurry. And now I can charge super fast when I'm on the go. So go ahead and check it out by using the links in the description and pin comment below. And that brings us to number four, which is the vapor chamber cooler only on the Pro models. And as I've been using these phones, especially the Pro Max, over the last 7 weeks, I've realized that for regular people, it doesn't really matter. So, I was testing gaming on the new iPhone 17, and I was running COD Mobile at 120 Hz, and it was insanely smooth. It felt just as smooth as it's always been on the Pro phones. The back wasn't heating up. It was just fine for all of my gaming needs. And that doesn't even mention just regular use. So I think the vapor chamber for regular people doesn't really matter. Only for those techies that push their phone to the limits with very long gaming sessions. Real difference number five is the USBC transfer speeds on the bottom. And this is a huge deal because the Pro models have it 20 times faster in terms of the speeds. you have basically 10 Gbits per second versus 480 megabits per second, 20 times more. And this matters so much to me because I shoot a lot of video content on my iPhone, whether it be just personally at home or for work sponsored videos. A lot of it's on my iPhone and I use an SSD like this. I just plug it right into my iPhone and I transfer all of the video clips within just moments. And then I plug that in to my Mac Studio for editing. If you do the same thing on this base iPhone 17, it takes forever. I mean, literally 20 times longer and it is extremely annoying. Every time I do it on this phone, I get annoyed. So that USBC being faster is a big deal. Moving on to number six. This is one that matters a lot to me as a video creative, and that is the better camera recording capabilities on the Pro models, specifically recording 4K at 120fps for that slow motion, which is so easy to edit in the photos app. You just adjust it, you choose what slow-mo, and it works great. We actually care a lot about 4K at 120 because we bought all three of our cameras, our front, our top, our side. They're all A7S3s from Sony because they support 4K at 120 just like the Pro models and it looks amazing. I use this all the time for nice B-roll shots of products that we do. It works great. Of course, you also have the ProRes that makes it even better for all that color depth. you have pro raw, you have the log, just better camera capabilities. I think that's a big deal for people who care about that. But of course, a lot of regular people don't. So, it seems like a lot of these updates are focused on pro users. And finally, for number seven, we have the battery life, which the base iPhone 17 is rated for 30 hours of video playback, 33 on the Pro, and 39 on the Pro Max. The thing is that the plus model is now gone. If you want a base iPhone 117, you cannot get it in a bigger size with a bigger battery. This is what you're limited to. So, if you want the craziest battery life like this, you got to pay for the Pro Max. And let me tell you, it is worth it because it lasts forever. And the most important feature of a tech device is always the battery because it lets you use all of those other features for longer without getting stuck with a dead phone. and that matters so much to me and that is why I just can't get over this Pro Max model. So, with all that said, after 7 weeks, it's surprising how little difference you now get with the Pro models versus the 17. I remember last year where you'd have a massive list of differences. And people were so disappointed with the iPhone 16, especially the Android community making fun of that 60 Hz display, making fun of no always on, making fun of the thick bezels. All of those reasons are gone and the Android community has now shut up. They can't say anything bad about the 17 because it is such a killer value. So, Apple has been absolutely killing it with these phones and I love just all those pro features you can get on the Pro Max. Let me know out of those seven that I talked about, which one matters the most to you down in the comments section below. Thanks for watching and we'll see you in the next video. [Music]

title: iPhone 17 Pro / Max - Best Camera Settings
transcript: In this video, I'm going to show you how to set up your iPhone camera to shoot the best photos and the best videos. One of the key camera settings on your iPhone for getting the best quality photos and videos is choosing between high efficiency and most compatible. High efficiency is what I use because you get the same quality, but the files are smaller and therefore they're less demanding on your phone's storage. High efficiency saves your photos in the HIF format. And this is more advanced and more efficient than the standard old JPEG. But the downside is that some platforms and some apps don't allow you to upload in this format. For example, if you try to upload an HIF photo to Reddit, it's not going to let you. But if you share directly from your iPhone to Reddit or other platforms, iOS automatically converts these photos to the good old common [music] JPEG. So you might think about choosing most compatible here instead. And then your photos are going to be saved as JPEG files directly. And that's, you know, much more universally compatible. However, there are trade-offs because your videos are going to be saved with the older, less efficient H.264 264 codec instead of the modern HEVC or otherwise known as H.265 codec. And as well, if you want to record Dolby Vision HDR, this is only possible using the high efficiency H.265 codec. Now, unfortunately, the iPhone's iOS doesn't let you set photo and video formats independently. You know, whichever one of these you choose is going to be applied to both photo and video formats together. So, this means that if you want to shoot high efficiency video, but save your photos as JPEG, you're going to need to toggle between the setting manually each time. So, obviously, nobody is actually going to do that. But here is a little tip. So, you can actually have your iPhone convert HIF photos to JPEGs when you send them via AirDrop. So, after you tap the AirDrop button, before you send, just tap options and then choose most compatible. when the photo arrives at its destination, it's going to be converted to the JPEG. So, it's not a perfect solution, but it might help you out in some cases. Now, bear in mind that some photo editing features in your iPhone are only available for photos shot using the HIF format, for example, if you want to edit style and tone. So, all that considered, my overall advice is just to choose high efficiency here, unless basically you just want an easier life and you're not bothered about your files being smaller or the loss of certain features. Dolby Vision was introduced to iPhones by Apple over 5 years ago now. But despite that, we rarely see iPhone videos shared on social media in Dolby Vision. And this is going to be turned on by default if you have a new iPhone. So Dolby Vision is a high dynamic range video format that dynamically adjusts brightness, color, and contrast. title: I Used the iPhone 17 Pro Deep Blue for a Week - Here’s What Surprised Me
transcript: So, the iPhone 17 Pro just came out, and of course, I had to get my hands on it. I've been using it for about over a week now, and I want to give you my honest thoughts, not just about the specs, but what it's actually like to live with this thing dayto-day? Because let's be real, we all know iPhones are expensive. The 17 Pro starts at $1,100. So, the big question is, is it worth upgrading or should you just stick with whatever phone you have? I've been using the iPhone 15 Pro for 2 years, and it's been a solid experience. titanium frame, great performance, and a polished overall feel, it's one of those phones that really made me feel Apple took a big step forward. Now, the iPhone 17 Pro is out, and while it brings some interesting upgrades, I want to explore whether it actually warrants an upgrade, especially for someone like me who already owns a recent iPhone. Apple's strategy in recent years has been to refine rather than reinvent. With the 7 Pro, that refinement continues. The phone features the new A19 Pro chip, improved cameras, and a few design tweaks. On paper, it mounts sound impressive, but when you look at it in the hand and consider your daily use, the question becomes, does it feel like a true step forward, or is it more of a beauty update? In this review, I'll walk you through the iPhone 17 Pro from the perspective of someone who uses it in everyday life, covering the design, display, cameras, performance, battery, and other features while keeping comparisons to the iPhone 15 Pro and occasionally referencing to the 16 Pro for context. By the end, you'll have a clear picture of whether the iPhone 17 Pro is the right choice for you. Apple has always emphasized design as one of the main differentiators for the iPhone, and the 17 Pro continues that tradition. The phone comes in silver, cosmic orange, and deep blue finishes. Apple claims a premium feel with a ceramic shield front and back and an aluminum frame. In theory, this should deliver a very sturdy and luxurious feel. However, personally, the design of the iPhone 17 Pro doesn't feel as premium to me as the 15 Pro did. While the build is solid, the overall aesthetic feels slightly more industrial and less refined. It's not a drastic downgrade, but it's enough to notice when holding it side by side with the 15 Pro. The edges are slightly more rounded, and the back finish doesn't have quite the same visual depth as the previous models. Of course, this is subjective. Some people will prefer the new look, but for me, the design doesn't inspire an upgrade. The size is also something to consider. The 17 Pro is slightly larger than the 15 Pro, and the Pro Max is noticeably bigger. If you prefer a phone that feels sleek and compact, the size difference may be relevant. Apple still maintains IP68 water and dust resistance, which reassuring. The materials themselves are environmentally conscious using recycled aluminum and other components. Overall, the 17 Pro has a strong, durable feel, but lacks the same premium wow factor that the 14, 15, and 16 Pro had when it first launched. For someone upgrading from an older iPhone, it will still feel like a very highquality device. For someone like me, the design alone isn't compelling enough to justify an upgrade. The display on the iPhone 17 Pro is a 6.3 in OLED panel, slightly larger than the 15 Pro with a resolution of 2,622x 1,26 pixels. It features Apple's promotion technology with a refresh rate up to 120 Hz, HDR support, and the always on display feature introduced in recent iPhones. For daily use, the display is bright, sharp, and vibrant. Streaming videos, browsing, social media, or reading text is smooth thanks to the high refresh rate. The color accuracy is excellent, and the true tone ensures that whites don't feel harsh in different lighting conditions. The display is also very responsive, which makes navigating the phone and typing a pleasure. Compared to the 16 Pro, the 17 Pro's display is marginally brighter and slightly more refined, but not dramatically different. Users of the 15 Pro may notice some improvements, but again, it's not a revolutionary change. The always on display is convenient, showing notifications and time without needing to pick up the phone, but for some people, it might not feel essential. Apple has also retained the dynamic island, which continues to serve as a functional and aesthetic centerpiece of the top screen. Overall, the 17 Pro display is excellent and more than good for most users. It's polished, fast, and enjoyable to use, but it's not a reason alone to upgrade if you already have a recent iPhone. The camera system on the iPhone 17 Pro is arguably one of the most talked about features. Apple has made some upgrades across the board, focusing on resolution, versatility, and computational photography. The main sensor is a 48 megapixel Pro Fusion camera with sensor shift stabilization. It performs exceptionally well in various lighting conditions, capturing detailed and vibrant images. There is also a 48 meap ultrawide camera with a 120 degree field of view and a 48 meap fusion telephoto lens that enables 4x optical zoom for everyday users. This means sharper zoomed in shots and more flexibility when framing photos. The front camera has also been upgraded to 18 megapixels with autofocus improving selfies and video calls especially in low light. Video recording supports up to 4K Dolby Vision at multiple frame rates, ProRes video, and a new action mode for smoother handheld video. And it's not just that. The front camera now supports center stage, so it can follow you around and keep you centered during FaceTime calls. Plus, you can even record using the front and back cameras at the same time, which makes it perfect for quick vlogs or reaction style clips. For casual photography, the iPhone 17 Pro is more than enough. It's fantastic. On the front, you get an 18 megapixel selfie camera with Apple's new square sensor. What that means is you can switch between portrait and landscape framing without rotating the phone. Portraits look professional, night mode is reliable, and the ultra wide lens allows for creative shots. Compared to the iPhone 15 Pro, the differences are noticeable in zoom performance and low light detail. Compared to the 16 Pro, the upgrades are very small, mainly refining the existing camera capabilities rather than reinventing them. For me personally, while the camera system is impressive, it doesn't make me feel the need to upgrade from the 15 Pro. The 15 Pro cameras are already excellent for everyday use, and the 17 Pro offers mostly refinements rather than transformative changes. The A19 Pro chip powers the new iPhone 17 Pro, featuring a six core CPU, a six core GPU, and a 16 core noral engine. Apple claims it delivers f has the performance and improved efficiency compared to the A17 chip in the 15 Pro. In practical terms, the 17 Pro is extremely fast. Apps open instantly, multitasking is smooth, and even intensive games run without lag. For mobile gaming, the performance improvements are noticeably, especially in graphically demanding titles. Frame rates are stable, loading times are minimal, and the phone handles high resolution textures with ease. Apple also highlights hardwarebased rate tracing for more realistic lighting effects in games, but for most users, this isn't a major factor. Casual gaming, social media, streaming, and productivity apps all run effortlessly, and even heavy multitasking doesn't slow the phone down. Compared to the 15 Pro, performance is slightly faster, but the differences are very subtle for average users. The 15 Pro already handles almost everything without hesitation. So, unless you're a mobile gamer pushing the limits or a power user needing every ounce of performance, the A19 Pro is impressive, but not essential. Battery life is one of the strongest points of the iPhone 17 Pro. Up to 33 hours of video playback on the 17 Pro and 39 hours on the Pro Max. For everyday use, this translates to easily getting through a full day and often longer depending on your usage patterns. Compared to the 15 Pro, the 17 Pro offers noticeably improvements. Streaming, gaming, and heavy camera use are slightly less draining, meaning fewer midday charges. For people who rely on their phone all day without convenient access to a charger, this is a significant benefit. Charging options have been also refined. Fast charging can get the phone to 50% in 20 to 30 minutes, depending on the adapter, and Max wireless charging is still supported up to 25 watts. Apple has also upgraded USBC support to faster data transfer speeds, which is great if you often offload photos or video files. For many users, improved battery life may be one of the main reasons to consider upgrading, especially if your current phone struggles to last a full day. However, if your recent iPhone still delivers solid battery performance, the difference may not be dramatic enough to justify an immediate upgrade. The iPhone 17 Pro includes all the connectivity and durability features expected from a premium smartphone. 5G support is standard. Wi-Fi 7 ensures faster wireless speeds. And Bluetooth 6 improves device pairing and stability. The phone continues to use Face ID and has an updated ultra wideband chip for precise location tracking and airdrop improvements. Apple also emphasizes environmental responsibility using recycled materials in the chassis and packaging. While these may not affect your daily experience, it reflects Apple's ongoing focus on sustainability. On the software side, the 17 Pro ships with iOS 26, which introduces subtle refinements across the system, but nothing that feels revolutionary for some users coming from the 15 or 16 Pro. Overall, these features maintain the phone's premium feel, but again, they don't provide a compelling reason to upgrade unless you're several generations behind. So, where does it leave the iPhone 17 Pro? It's an excellent phone. The cameras are versatile and capable. Performance is fast, battery life is strong, and the overall user experience is polished. Apple has refined the iPhone in meaningful ways. That said, for someone like me already using the iPhone 15 Pro, the improvements feel like a very small step up. The design doesn't resonate really as premium. The performance gains are noticeably but not game-changing. And the camera upgrades, while impressive, are not transformative for everyday use. Essentially, it's a refinement rather than a revolution. For users on older iPhones, say the 13 or earlier, the 17 Pro could be a very compelling upgrade. The improved cameras, battery life, and performance would feel like significant steps forward. For users already on the 14, 15, or 16 Pro, the case for upgrading is weaker unless you are drawn specifically to the new camera features or slightly improved battery life. In conclusion, the iPhone 17 Pro is a polished and powerful device, but it may not be the upgrade that everyone is looking for. It's a great phone for those seeking the latest features, but for existing recent iPhone users, the small nature of the changes means that sticking with your current model is a reasonable choice. That's my review of the iPhone 17 Pro. I'll be posting some camera comparisons video, so make sure you're subscribed if you want to see that. And I also ordered the Apple Watch 11, so there are a lot of videos coming soon. And if you have questions or want me to test something specific, drop it in the comments. I actually read them all. Thanks for watching, and I'll see you in the next one.

title: Xiaomi 17 Pro Max review
transcript: [music] Xiaomi is out to take on the iPhones with their newest flagship, the Xiaomi 17 Pro Max. This phone has a lot to offer, but still does it have what it takes to compete against the other Pro Max out there? I'm will go for GSM Marina and let's find out in our Xiaomi 17 Pro Max review. Xiaomi has switched up its naming scheme this time around coming out with the Xiaomi 17 Pro Max. Sounds pretty similar to another popular flagship. However, this Pro Max is quite different and not only because it's an Android phone. The island on the back is not just for the cameras. It houses a whole second screen. There are a bunch of other nice features here, too, including a large main display, a cuttingedge chipset, a huge battery, fast charging, and a high-end camera setup, which includes a five time zoom, and a new selfie camera. Before we move on, I should mention that for now, this phone is only sold in the Chinese market, and we bought it directly from there. Plans about global availability aren't clear yet, but you can get the phone as a gray import if you're willing to make some concessions, which we'll get to in a bit. Besides that second screen, the looks of the Xiaomi Pro Max are quite similar to what you might expect from an iPhone. There's a flat back made of fiberglass and a flat aluminum frame, all with a matte finish that's hard to smudge. And despite the phone's large battery, it doesn't feel bulky and it weighs about the same as other current flagships. You get IP68 rated protection here, and it's better than usual, rated against water submersion to a depth of 6 m. Let's go ahead and see what this second screen is all about. It's an LTPO OLED screen with a 120 Hz refresh rate like you get on the front. Just this one is small at 2.9 in. It provides plenty of functionality. One thing it can do is alert you for a call and you can answer the call directly from this screen itself. In general, you get something like your typical lock screen with a clock and access to notifications. You can customize this screen with your own photos or text to make it yours. Or you can take a screenshot of anything at all, like an image or some text, and pin that onto the second screen. There's also support for a number of animated pets powered by AI. They're pretty cute and bound to turn some heads. The secondary screen can be used to show timers. You can control them from here, too. And when you play certain media, like songs on Spotify, you get info and album covers and of course media controls, There is some support for gaming on this screen, too. But it requires you to use a specific custom phone case with buttons to use as controls. But perhaps the most useful of this screen's functions is the ability to control the rear cameras with an in-depth camera interface. You can switch between the three rear cameras, go into portrait or photo collage modes, and adjust camera settings. This viewfinder can also be used if you're taking a picture of someone else so they can see themselves while posing in the frame. Moving on to the 17 Pro Max's main screen. Like I mentioned, it's again an LTPO OLED, but this time it's a 6.9 in one protected by Xiaomi's Dragon Crystal 3 glass. You get thin bezels, great sharpness, and features like 12-bit color depth and support for HDR10 Plus and Dolby Vision video playback, too. Both the front and rear panels are matched when it comes to brightness, and the performance is solid. In manual mode, we recorded a little over 600 nits, which was able to boost to over 1,020 nits in automatic brightness mode. And it was able to get even brighter than that in smaller areas of the screen. And with a 120 Hz refresh rate, swiping and scrolling on both of the 17 Pro Max's displays is quite smooth to the eye. And thanks to LTPOT, this refresh rate can dial down as low as one herz when the screen is idling to save power. For audio, the phone has a pair of stereo speakers. They earn a score of good for loudness, and the audio quality here is some of the best we've heard. Tracks are clean and full sounding with prominent bass and crisp vocals. You can listen for yourself with our comparison tool linked below. You can wake up and unlock the phone with an ultrasonic underdis fingerprint reader, and it's reliable and snappy. The Xiaomi 17 Pro Max offers 512 gigs of base storage, or you could get it with a terabyte on board. The interface of the 17 Pro Max is Xiaomi's latest Hyper OS 3.0 based on Android 16. If you've used a recent version of Hyper OS, you'll feel right at home as the visual changes are pretty subtle. However, Xiaomi does claim that the animations are smoother. Now, the most noticeable UI related change is the super island, which is something like the dynamic island you see on the iPhones. It gives you contextual information and quick access to certain supported apps that are running in the background. Xiaomi has improved its connectivity features as well. Previously, you could only share things like files with other Xiaomi devices logged into the same Xiaomi account, but now there's support for sharing with non Xiaomi devices, too. Of course, as we're reviewing the Chinese version of the Xiaomi 17 Pro Max, there are some China only apps here, which you wouldn't find on a global version. Also, the phone only has English and Chinese languages, and you don't get things like the Google discover feed, location history, and Google Maps, quick share between phones, Android Auto, or voice activation for the Google Assistant. But still, other than that, there is compatibility here with Google services. You can easily get the Play Store and use it to download all the other apps you're used to. Google Gemini, Google Wallet, and Google Maps work fine for the most part, and there are even tutorials online on how to enable Circle to Search. So, at the end of the day, you can have something similar to what you may be used to here in the West, but you need to set it up, and some features may be missing here and there. The Xiaomi 17 Pro Max is one of the first phones to come out with the new generation chipset from Qualcomm, the Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5. It promises much improved CPU and graphics performance, including rateracing, plus better AI processing and more power efficiency. In benchmarks, we do see some significant improvements over flagships running on a Snapdragon 8 Elite, but we expected the gap to be larger, and it actually is larger in other new flagships running on the same new hardware. Still, while it seems that the 17 Pro Max isn't performing to its full potential, this is one of the most powerful flagships out there right now. The thermal management here is about what we'd expect from a flagship. We observed significant thermal throttling during our prolonged CPU stress test to about 50% of peak performance. On to battery life, and the Xiaomi 17 Pro Max should be great thanks to its huge 7500 mAh battery. Indeed, the battery life here is excellent. Some of the best around. The phone earned an active use score of 20 hours and 50 minutes, which is very impressive. The 17 Pro Max supports 100 watt wired charging either with Xiaomi's proprietary charger or power delivery charger with PBS. The charging speed is quite fast, especially considering the size of the battery. With the Xiaomi charger that came with the phone, a full charge took only 39 minutes, and charging with the standard USB power delivery charger was only marginally slower. There's also support for 50 watt fast wireless charging here. That was only with a proprietary charger. Now, we have the cameras. The rear setup includes a main cam, a five times telephoto zoom, and an ultrawide with autofocus, all at 50 megapixels. The overall quality of the main camera during the day is great. You get sharp, detailed photos with no noise and a wide dynamic range, and the performance indoors is pretty solid, too. However, the post-processing has its quirks. Some shots could use more contrast, especially in more challenging lighting conditions. Additionally, there's a strong tendency to oversaturate the reds and sometimes the greens. The phone does a good job at rendering skin textures, and the defocus backgrounds in portrait mode are convincing looking, too. Two times digital zoom from the main cam looks nice with a minimal loss of sharpness. Some objects with the fine detail in the distance may appear fuzzier, but that's part for the course. The main camera's lowlight photos come out excellent. The phone exposes well and delivers well-developed shadows and highlights and absolutely no noise. The contrast could be a bit higher in some of the photos, but that's just a nitpick. The telephoto camera matches the main camera's processing overall, but fails to impress when it comes to quality in general. Many of the photos we took were either blurry or fuzzy, suggesting that the OIS is not doing a great job. Shots of people look great though, and the portrait mode again shows some excellent subject separation from the artificially defocused background. The telephoto camera can also focus as close as 30 cm, and it's able to snap some nicel looking close-ups. The 10 times crops from the five times telephoto are a bit fuzzier, but they generally look okay. The five times telephoto shots at night won't hold up to close scrutiny as they're not terribly sharp. However, they have good dynamic range and a clean rendition with no noise. The color temperature is once again too warm. The ultrawide camera is more than decent during the day. Sharpness and detail are just okay, but the dynamic range is pretty wide, maybe even above average for an ultrawide shooter. However, with a focal length of 17 mm, the field of view is not as wide as expected. It's not so far away from the main cameras, which is already on the wider side. The ultrawide camera's low light photos are not very competitive. They lack fine detail. Sharpness isn't up to snuff even for an ultra wide, and the color temperature is off just like on the other two cameras. This year, Xiaomi has included a new 50 megapixel front-facing camera for selfies. The subject's face is always well exposed, and there's plenty of detail. But why use the front-facing camera when you can use the display on the back to take selfies with the rear cameras? These shots taken with the main camera come out excellent with great detail and a naturally blurred background. Two time zoom shots look good, too, if you want a closer framing. We're not too impressed by the ultra wide camera's quality in this use case, but will get the job done if you want to fit more people into the frame. The Pro Max supports up to 8K video recording with this main camera, while the rest of the cameras cap at 4K at 60 fps. and you can shoot in 10- bit log if you want to do the color grading yourself. The main camera's performance with video capture is pretty solid in both the day and the night. In both cases, you get excellent fine detail, good sharpness, great dynamic range, and virtually no noise even in the low light video. We only wish the stabilization was a bit better. The five zoom video looks okay. Stabilization is good, but the clips have lower contrast than expected and can be generally fuzzy. We are happy with how the low light footage comes out though. The autofocus is not the best, but the dynamic range is great. The daylight ultrawide video is generally good as far as ultrawides go. There's adequate sharpness and contrast, but the low light ultra wide video is hard to recommend. It has limited dynamic range and it's noisy and way too soft. Video stabilization when walking is great in contrast to the somewhat shaky footage you get when shooting handheld from a stationary position or when panning. So, there you have it. The Xiaomi 17 Pro Max. It ticks a lot of boxes with its solid build quality, amazing speakers, and great displays, including that unique second screen. The chipset is cutting edge, and you get both excellent battery life and super fast charging. Our only issue is with the cameras. Sure, they're good, but they're not as good as the competition. So, if you're after the ultimate camera phone, you might be better off elsewhere. But if that's not your priority, the Xiaomi 17 Pro Max is a well-rounded flagship that's really worth its salt. So much so that it might be one of the few phones worth gray importing if you don't want to wait around and see if there's going to be a global version. Thanks for watching, guys. If you're looking for alternatives, you can check out our reviews of the iPhone 17 Pro Max and the Oppo FindX 9 Pro. Let us know what you think and I'll see you on the next one.

title: How to Check Call History on iPhone 17 Pro, 17 Pro Max, 16, 15 in iOS 26
transcript: Here's a quick tutorial on how to check incoming and outgoing call history on the iPhone phone app. First, open the phone app on your iPhone. Tap the calls tab and you're going to see your recent calls in the list. If the list looks limited and you want to check the old call history of a specific person saved in your contacts, here's what to do. Search and find that contact using the search option at the bottom right corner. Tap to open the contact profile, and you're going to see the call history right under the details section. Here's the full list of call history. That's it. This feature is available in the iOS 26 update. I hope you liked this video. If you've got any questions, drop a comment below. Don't forget to like, share, and subscribe for new free tech guide video updates.

title: iPhone 17 Pro Hidden Treasure: Open Gate Video & ProRes Raw | 4K HDR video
transcript: Hello, I am Jan from Prague, the Czech Republic  and today I want to show you a amazing new feature   of the new iPhone 17 Pro. I can now record video  using full size of the sensor in ProRes RAW,   which means that I do not have to stress  anymore about things not being straight   enough because I can easily fix it and crop it  later, which makes shooting much more easier. Now if we are in the default camera app of iPhone  17 Pro, I am without changing the position of   tripod over the camera. I'm able to change whether  it's horizontal or vertical orientation and it's   quite interesting, quite tempting. You can see I  can turn on or turn off option to get some more   people into the frame while I will not be using  it. What I want you to see is the app called   Final Cut Camera which is right here and where all  the magic can happen. Now I can turn on Apple   ProRes RAW. But if I do it, the iPhone tells me  that I need external disc which I have. So it's   fine. I just need to turn it on. And here we go.  Here the magic can happen. And now we got what we   really wanted to get. So I am able to use the data  from the full sensor and it can be cropped. It's   really useful. If I turn off that simulation,  you now see how the raw would look without the   preview. So it's certainly better to keep the  lot preview on. Here we go. And now it looks   uh of course much better for the everyday  use. I have to admit I was really afraid   that I will have to do a lot of editing. But  the Final Cut Pro on desktop is really able to   work with the raw file. So I didn't really have  to edit it much. I had to do some small things,   small changes, but I will probably be able to  leave it as it was, which is amazing. And of   course you are able to open your raw footage  here in the final cut pro on desktop and you   are able to use that function Modify ProRess RAW  settings even before you start a proper editing.   So you are able to set the processing here. You  are even able to reset ISO or Exposure bias or   White balance which is amazing because you can  play with this and only then you would go to the   proper editing tools. Just one important note I  was really surprised when I've noticed that the 16   minutes of the shot of the opening shot took more  than 100 GB which is a huge load of data.   That's why I understand why it is essential to  have the external camera because without that   you would just not be able to do anything and  yes I am in the selfie mode and I have to be   honest I would probably not be using that much.  So typically I would just touch this button and   turn the normal mode and I would be using  that Open Gate stuff with what you see here it   means with the main camera system which is quite  interesting. So if we sum it up in one sentence,   the open gate gives us a really quite important  tool. So thank you so much for your attention. I   hope I gave you some inspiration. Take care.  I'm Jan from Prague, the Czech Republic.

title: Why iPhone 17 Pro Is Turning Pink 😳 | Apple’s Hidden Problem Explained
transcript: The brand new iPhone 17 Pro, Apple's most advanced, most premium device yet. But wait, users around the world are now saying their shiny orange iPhones are turning pink. Yes, the color is literally changing on its own. So, what's going on? Is this Apple's biggest design flaw in years, or something deeper happening under the surface? Let's find out. In Apple's latest lineup, the cosmic orange or sunny orange iPhone 17 Pro was meant to stand out. a bold premium luxury color. But only weeks after launch, social media exploded with pictures of these phones turning pinkish or rose gold under sunlight. At first, people thought it was fake or photo edited until dozens of users confirmed the same thing was happening to them. Here's the truth. The iPhone 17 Pro doesn't use titanium like the older Pro models. Instead, Apple switched to anodized aluminum, a lightweight metal that gets its color from a chemical dye process. The problem that color sits only a few microns deep in the metal surface. When it's exposed to UV sunlight, heat, or certain cleaning chemicals, that dye starts to oxidize. The orange pigment fades away, leaving a pinkish tint behind. In simple words, it's not paint peeling off. It's chemistry doing its job a little too well. Apple actually warns users not to use bleach, hydrogen peroxide, or alcohol-based wipes on anodized aluminum surfaces, but many people still clean their phones with regular sanitizing wipes. And that's where things go wrong. Those chemicals react with the metal coating, eating away the outer protective seal, and boom, your orange turns into pink. Now, here's the interesting part. Some experts believe this isn't just sunlight or cleaning. They say a few batches of the Cosmic Orange model had a weak sealing process during manufacturing. That means the protective layer that locks the color inside wasn't fully bonded, making those units far more sensitive to oxidation and fading. And guess what? Apple hasn't officially admitted this issue yet. If you already own a sunny orange iPhone 17 Pro, here's how to keep it safe. Avoid direct sunlight exposure for long hours. Never use chemical cleaners, only a soft, damp cloth. Use a UV protective case. And if your phone is already changing color, contact Apple support immediately. Some users have reportedly received replacement units under warranty. Apple is known for design perfection. Every millimeter, every material is tested to its limit. But sometimes even the most advanced technology can be defeated by something as simple as the sun. This isn't just a color issue. It's a reminder that innovation always comes with risks.
